methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $300M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 9 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Robotics & Manufacturing. Range: $300M - $10.0B.

private.name: Hadrian
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C), post-money $1.6B

private.name: Nimble Robotics
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B

data.narrative:
Bright Machines, a Series C robotics and manufacturing automation company founded by Amar Hanspal and Lior Susan, has raised $300M+ to date. Valuation analysis draws from a peer set of nine same-sector and adjacent-stage companies, comprising eight private firms (including Hadrian at $1.6B, 1X Technologies at $10.0B, and Exotec at $2.0B) and one public comparable.

Two triangulation methods are applicable. The stage-progression method applies a 1.7x multiplier to the $300M total raised proxy, implying a $510M valuation. The peer-median method references the median valuation across the nine-company Robotics & Manufacturing cohort, which stands at $1.6B, with a range from $300M to $10.0B. This wider distribution reflects heterogeneity within the frontier-tech robotics space, particularly variance driven by relative product maturity and go-to-market traction.

Synthesizing both methods yields a triangulated range of $434M (low) to $1.8B (high), with a median of $1.1B. The stage-progression method anchors the lower bound, reflecting a conservative capital-multiple approach; peer median anchors the upper bound. The wide range reflects material uncertainty regarding Bright Machines' relative positioning on product commercialization, customer concentration, and capital efficiency versus named peers. Key downside risks include execution risk in manufacturing automation. Upside hinges on demonstrated unit economics and customer concentration patterns within the cohort.
